questions.question: Who among the following sent a telegram on 6 August 1857 stating, 'Our men are cowed by the numbers opposed to them and the endless fighting. Every village is held against us, the zamindars have risen to oppose us'?
questions.solution:
Lieutenant Colonel Tytler

On 6 August 1857, Lieutenant Colonel Tytler sent a telegram to the Commander-in-Chief expressing the sheer scale of the 1857 Revolt. His message highlighted that the British were not just fighting mutinous soldiers, but a collective uprising involving zamindars and entire villages, which had 'cowed' the British troops.

questions.question: In which of the following cities was the Paramhansa Mandali founded to work for the abolition of caste?
questions.solution:
Paramhansa Mandali

The Paramhansa Mandali was founded in 1849 in Bombay (Mumbai) by Dadoba Pandurang and Durgaram Mehtaji. It was a secret society that aimed at the abolition of caste. At its meetings, members from different castes consumed food cooked by lower-caste individuals to demonstrate their break from orthodox traditions.

questions.question: In which of the following years did the Government of Bengal pass the Calcutta Electric Lighting Act?
questions.solution:
Calcutta Electric Lighting Act

The Calcutta Electric Lighting Act was passed in 1895. This legislation was a prerequisite for the commercial distribution of electricity, leading to the establishment of the Calcutta Electric Supply Corporation (CESC) and the eventual lighting up of the city by 1899.

questions.question: Which of the following transportation systems was primarily developed in India to serve colonial economic interests?
questions.solution:
Railways and Colonial Interest

The Railways were primarily developed by the British to serve their own economic and military interests. It allowed for the rapid movement of troops to suppress revolts and the efficient transport of raw materials from the Indian hinterland to ports for export to Britain.

questions.question: Which of the following subjects was NOT a part of the Transferred Subjects under the Government of India Act of 1919?
questions.solution:
Transferred vs. Reserved Subjects

Under the Dyarchy system of the 1919 Act, Police was a 'Reserved Subject', meaning it remained under the direct control of the Governor and his executive council. 'Transferred Subjects' like Health, Education, and Local Self-Government were overseen by Indian ministers.

questions.question: In the context of British plantations in India, what does the term ‘bigha’ refer to?
questions.option_1: A revenue estate which has one or more villages
questions.option_2: An agreement made with the ryots
questions.option_3: A plant that replaced indigo in Britain
questions.option_4: A unit of measurement of land
questions.solution:
The Bigha

In British India, a bigha was a standard unit of measurement of land. Before the British standardized it (often to one-third of an acre in Bengal), the size of a bigha varied significantly from region to region.

questions.question: Who among the following passed an Act in 1898, making it an offence to provoke people against the British rulers?
questions.solution:
Sedition Act of 1898

Lord Curzon is associated with the passage of an Act in 1898 (amending the Indian Penal Code) that made it a serious offence to provoke people against the British rulers. This was part of a broader effort to suppress the rising influence of nationalist newspapers and orators.

questions.question: Who among the following strongly criticised British education for being too Western and disconnected from Indian realities?
questions.solution:
Rabindranath Tagore’s Criticism

Rabindranath Tagore was a strong critic of the colonial education system, which he viewed as a 'factory' for producing clerks. He believed it was disconnected from Indian nature and heritage, leading him to establish Santiniketan, where education was provided in a natural, creative environment.

questions.question: Which organisation was established in 1817 to promote Western education among Indians?
questions.solution:
Hindoo College

Established in 1817 in Calcutta, Hindoo College (now Presidency University) was founded by Raja Rammohan Roy, David Hare, and Radhakanta Deb. It was one of the earliest institutions to provide Western-style secular education in English to the Indian youth.

questions.question: He was a militant nationalist who believed that the only way to India’s freedom is through mass protests, especially by the youth. For this purpose, he began celebrating local festivals like Ganesh Chaturthi and Shivaji Jayanti on a grand scale, urging the youth to join in. He was also the editor of ‘Kesari’, a Marathi newspaper, which became one of the strongest critics of British rule. The leader in question is:
questions.solution:
Bal Gangadhar Tilak

Bal Gangadhar Tilak used cultural symbols to mobilize the masses. He transformed the Ganesh Chaturthi and Shivaji Jayanti festivals into public nationalist events. Through his newspaper Kesari, he criticized British policies and popularized the demand for self-rule.

questions.question: Raja Ram Mohan Roy was an active social reformer who spoke greatly for the problems faced by women. He was an inseparable agent who brought down the social evil of ‘sati’. The reform association founded by him was:
questions.solution:
Brahmo Samaj

Raja Rammohan Roy founded the Brahmo Samaj in 1828. This reformist association campaigned against social evils like Sati, idolatry, and the caste system, while promoting monotheism and the upliftment of women through education and legal rights.

questions.question: In which of the following years did Edward, Prince of Wales visit India on behalf of his father King George V, to thank the nation for the essential role it had played during the First World War?
questions.solution:
Prince of Wales’ Visit

The Prince of Wales (the future Edward VIII) visited India in 1921. His visit was met with widespread boycotts and strikes (hartals) organized by the Congress as part of the Non-Cooperation Movement, despite the official reason being to thank India for its WWI contributions.

questions.question: Who introduced the Permanent Settlement in British India?
questions.solution:
Permanent Settlement

Lord Cornwallis introduced the Permanent Settlement (also known as the Zamindari System) in Bengal and Bihar in 1793. It fixed the land revenue 'permanently,' making the zamindars the legal owners of the land as long as they paid the fixed tax to the British.

questions.question: During the Revolt of 1857, Rani Laxmibai led the rebellion from:
questions.solution:
Rani Laxmibai and Jhansi

Rani Laxmibai, the 'Jhansi ki Rani,' became a symbol of resistance when she led the rebellion from Jhansi. She fought valiantly against British forces after the Doctrine of Lapse was used to annex her state following the death of her husband.

questions.question: Gandhiji gave a call for a satyagraha against the Rowlatt Act in which of the following years?
questions.solution:
Rowlatt Satyagraha

Mahatma Gandhi launched a nationwide Satyagraha against the Rowlatt Act in 1919. It was his first major all-India struggle, utilizing hartals and peaceful protests, though it was tragically met with the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre in Amritsar.

questions.question: The Faraizi Movement was a religious reform movement of the 19ᵗʰ century in _______.
questions.solution:
Faraizi Movement

The Faraizi Movement was a 19th-century Islamic reform movement founded by Haji Shariatullah in Bengal. It initially focused on religious purification but evolved into a socio-economic struggle for the rights of peasantry against oppressive landlords.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a consequence of the Revolt of 1857?
questions.option_1: Lands and properties of Muslims were confiscated en masse.
questions.option_2: Policies that favoured zamindars and landlords were struck down.
questions.option_3: The strength of Indian soldiers in the army was reduced.
questions.option_4: The Governor-General of India was given the title 'Viceroy'.
questions.solution:
Consequences of the 1857 Revolt

A key consequence was the transfer of power from the East India Company to the British Crown, and the Governor-General was given the title of Viceroy. The British also adopted a policy of 'Divide and Rule,' often targeting Muslims, but they strengthened (not struck down) policies favoring loyal zamindars to ensure future support.

questions.question: What does the term ‘satyagraha’ primarily mean in Gandhian philosophy for mass mobilisation and India’s struggle for freedom during colonial rule?
questions.solution:
Satyagraha: Truth-Force

In Gandhian philosophy, Satyagraha translates literally to 'Truth-force' or 'Soul-force.' It is a method of non-violent resistance where the practitioner seeks to convert the opponent through the power of truth and self-suffering rather than physical force.

questions.question: During the colonial period, the manufacturing sector accounted for only ______.
questions.solution:
Manufacturing in the Colonial Era

During the colonial period, the manufacturing sector was severely underdeveloped and accounted for only about 10% of India's GDP. The economy was heavily tilted toward agriculture and the extraction of raw materials for British use.

questions.question: Who was the first Finance Minister of Independent India?
questions.solution:
R.K. Shanmugham Chetty

R.K. Shanmugham Chetty served as the first Finance Minister of Independent India. He presented the first budget of free India on 26 November 1947, laying the financial groundwork for the new nation.

questions.question: Which system of land tenure prevalent before independence led to exploitation of farmers?
questions.solution:
Zamindari System

The Zamindari system was the most exploitative land tenure system before independence. It created a class of intermediaries (landlords) who often extracted exorbitant rents from tenant farmers while having no incentive to improve the productivity of the land.

questions.question: Which of the following was the biggest armed resistance to colonialism in the 19ᵗʰ century in India?
questions.solution:
Sepoy Mutiny (1857)

The Sepoy Mutiny (also known as the First War of Independence) was the biggest armed resistance to British colonialism in 19th-century India. It united various sections of society, from soldiers to dispossessed royalty, in a massive effort to end British rule.

questions.question: Which reform movement in Kerala focused on the upliftment of the backward castes through education and social equality?
questions.solution:
SNDP Sabha

The Sree Narayana Dharma Paripalana (SNDP) Sabha was a reform movement in Kerala led by Sree Narayana Guru. It focused on the upliftment of the Ezhava community and other backward castes, famously advocating 'One Caste, One Religion, One God for Man'.

questions.question: Land revenue systems under British rule varied regionally. Which land revenue system was primarily followed in Bombay and Madras Presidencies?
questions.solution:
Ryotwari System

The Ryotwari system was the dominant land revenue system in the Madras and Bombay Presidencies. Unlike the Zamindari system, the government made settlements directly with the individual farmers (Ryots), who were recognized as the owners of the land.

questions.question: Who among the following laid the foundation stone of Indira Gandhi Rashtriya Uran Akademi, commonly called IGRUA, in 1985?
questions.solution:
IGRUAs Foundation

The foundation stone of the Indira Gandhi Rashtriya Uran Akademi (IGRUA), India's premier pilot training institute, was laid by Prime Minister Rajiv Gandhi in 1985 in Fursatganj, Uttar Pradesh.
